texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Herein, we introduce a novel membrane-based process for lithium recovery and compare it to the conventional solar evaporation followed by chemical precipitation process. Conventional technologies have limitations to meet the recent demand for massive lithium production due to several drawbacks of solar evaporation. Recently,  in  order  to  reduce  the  dependency  of  solar  evaporation,  several  technologies  have  been  proposed  such  as  precipitation,  ion-exchange,  liquid-liquid extraction, adsorption, and electrodialysis. We suggest a novel membrane-based lithium recovery process by combining membrane distillation (MD) and nanofiltration  (NF)  to  concentrate  a  brine  solution  containing  lithium  and  to  remove  divalent  ions.  The  proposed  membrane-based  process  was  demonstrated  to concentrate 100 ppm lithium solution in artificial brine to 1200 ppm lithium solution within several days and exhibited up to 60 times higher water flux (22.5 L m 2 h 1 ) than that of solar evaporation (0.37 L m 2  h 1 at 30 � C and 0.56 L m 2  h 1 at 50 � C). Moreover, the NF process can suppress crystal formation to prevent process failure while alleviating the massive chemical usage of the conventional process. As a result, the proposed membrane-based process showed a possibility to utilize the low concentration of lithium brine with one-tenth of capital cost, process time, and foot-print of the conventional process, and represented a competitive operating cost with the conventional process which can be reduced further by harnessing the waste heat from the industrial plants and solar energy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: During the last decade, the demand in the lithium-ion battery (LIB) industry for electric vehicles (EVs) and energy storage systems (ESS) has grown dramatically [1,2]. In particular, sales of EVs in 2020 are expected to be about 4 million, up from 1.4 million in 2017, and to expand to  21.5  million  by  2030,  indicating  24%  average  year-to-year  sales growth [3]. Energy storage systems using LIBs are also increasing in the smart grid and renewable energy systems [3]. Moreover, LIBs tend to dominate up to 90% of the total energy storage systems. In addition, people are struggling to find alternatives to lithium [4,5]. This increase in lithium demand for LIBs has led to sizeable changes in the price of lithium. Compared to the price of LIBs in January 2015, that in January 2018 increased about four times; from $50K per ton to $200K per ton [3].

texts.orig: However, as the cost of lithium has been skyrocketing over the last several years, the conventional lithium production process is not sufficient  to  satisfy  the  demands  of  the  lithium  industry  in  terms  of  productivity  and  price.  Solar  evaporation  process  has  limitations  as  it requires stringent climate conditions and a large footprint. In particular, solar  evaporation relies on the climate in a region and, thus, can be operated in only a few limited regions in South America despite high lithium reserves in other regions, such as in the United States and China. According to reports from the United States Geological Survey (USGS), weather-related complications, such as snowfall and downpours, have reduced lithium production [11,12]. Furthermore, the solar evaporation process requires a very long operating time (up to 24 months) to obtain a suitable  lithium  concentration.  To  replace  solar  evaporation,  various technologies have been reported for lithium recovery from brine where lithium concentration is low [13,14]. These include precipitation [15], ion-exchange  [16],  liquid-liquid  extraction  [17,18],  adsorption  [19], and electrodialysis [20]. However, until now, all these technologies still have some drawbacks such as a high investment cost, a massive chemical usage, and low system efficiency. Moreover, these technology still required a highly concentrated lithium-containing brines up to a few thousands of ppm of lithium prior to production [14,21]. Thus, a novel technology  for  enriching  the  low  lithium  concentration  to  high  concentration should be developed to substitute the solar evaporation.

texts.orig: Herein, we proposed a membrane-based lithium recovery process to solve these problems of the conventional solar evaporation process (see Fig. 1b). Membrane distillation (MD) and nanofiltration (NF) were used for  enriching  the  lithium-containing  brine  and  removing  undesirable ions such as calcium and magnesium resulting to alleviate the use of chemical  usage.  Thermodynamic  and  computational  simulation  was utilized  for  crystal  analysis  and  techno-economic  analysis  was  conducted to figure out the economic feasibility with conventional method.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lithium-containing artificial brine was prepared based on the content of representative lithium-containing brines with various kinds of salts  (see  Table  1).  Lithium  chloride  (LiCl),  sodium  chloride  (NaCl), sodium sulphate anhydrous (Na2SO4), magnesium sulphate hexahydrate (MgSO4 ⋅ 6H2O),  calcium  sulphate  anhydrous  (CaSO4),  boric  acid  (B (OH)3),  and  potassium  chloride  (KCl)  (all  chemicals  were  purchased from DaeJung Chem. Co. Ltd., Siheung, Republic of Korea) were dissolved in deionized water (D.I. water, Milli-Q ® Direct, Merck Millipore, Darmstadt, Germany). Commercial TFC NF membranes (NE 40, 70, and 90) and a hydrophobic porous membrane (GVHP) were produced by Toray  Chemicals  (Tokyo,  Japan)  and  Merck  Millipore  (Darmstadt, Germany), respectively.

texts.orig: The  surface  morphologies  of  membranes  were  analysed via  fi eldemission scanning  electron  microscope  (FE-SEM,  Hitachi,  Tokyo, Japan) with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) instrument. The dried samples were coated with Pt for 30 s with a Pt sputter (Hitachi E 1045, Tokyo, Japan). The chemical compositions of crystals were investigated using EDX, X-ray diffractometry (XRD) (SmartLab, Rigaku Corporation,  Tokyo,  Japan),  and  X-ray  photoelectron  spectroscopy (XPS)  (Theta  probe,  Thermo  Scientific,  MA,  USA)  to  determine  the species of crystals.

The PHREEQC software calculated the activity coefficient on the basis of the Pitzer approach which is suitable for high concentrated solution [23]. Input parameter is the concentration of the salts in the brine with other basic conditions such as temperature and pH. Then, PHREEQC returns the saturation index (SI) of all possible salts as following:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: SI ¼ IAP Ksp (1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where SI is saturation index, IAP is the ion activity product, Ksp  is the solubility product. If the SI is positive to the solution, then the solution is supersaturated with respect to that salt and precipitates as a crystal. If the SI is negative, then the solution is under-saturated, and salts can be dissolved. Therefore, according to the sign of the SI, it is possible to predict thermodynamically whether crystals will be precipitated in the solution.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.5. Solar evaporation test
texts.text: 2.5. Solar evaporation test

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To  compare  the  conventional  method  with  the  membrane-based process, a solar evaporation test was conducted on a lab-scale similar to those reported in other literatures [24,25]. As described in Scheme S1, a cuboid acrylic box (550 mm width, 500 mm length, and 350 mm height) was prepared. A ventilation fan was installed to maintain low relative humidity similar to the real environment. A lab-scale evaporation pan with a diameter of 23 cm and effective area of 0.0415 m 2 was prepared. The temperature and concentration of solution were controlled using a heat plate and recorded every 60 min with the ion conductivity meter. The temperature and relative humidity of the inside atmosphere  were  measured  with  a  thermo-hygrometer  and  ion  conductivity  meter.  The  water  flux  of  the  solar  evaporation  pond  was calculated based on the water level of the lab-scale evaporation pan.

texts.orig: A multi-cell, cross-flow apparatus was used to evaluate the NF performance of the membranes at 10 bar with a flow rate of 70 ml min 1 . The  effective  membrane  area  was  0.00062  m 2 .  The  membrane  was compacted with D.I. water at 10 bar overnight. After compaction, artificial lithium-containing brine was prepared as a feed solution, and NF performance was measured. Water flux was measured every 1 h, and 3 ml of samples were collected after 6 h to analyze solute rejection. The detailed procedure is described in our previous study [26].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.6.2. Membrane distillation performance
texts.text: 2.6.2. Membrane distillation performance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MD  performance  was  measured  using  a  lab-scale,  direct-contact, membrane  distillation (DCMD)  apparatus from Philos  Company (Guangmyeong, Republic of Korea). The effective membrane area was 0.00181 m 2 . A schematic diagram of the DCMD apparatus is presented in our previous study [27,28]. Temperatures of feed and permeate solutions were maintained at 50 o C and 20 o C, respectively, and measured at both the inlet and outlet of the cell. The operating pressure and flow rate of the feed and permeate solutions were measured. Artificial brines and D.I. water were prepared and used as feed and permeate solutions, respectively.  Water  flux  was  automatically  measured  from  the  overflowed permeate solution every 10 min. The conductivities of the bulk feed and permeate solutions were also recorded every 10 min to calculate salt rejection. Due to the limitations of the lab-scale MD apparatus, the rust from the apparatus needed to be washed out to prohibit pore clogging. In this study, the apparatus was washed with D.I. water after 100 h and then was tested for further concentration.
texts.text: MD  performance  was  measured  using  a  lab-scale,  direct-contact, membrane  distillation (DCMD)  apparatus from Philos  Company (Guangmyeong, Republic of Korea). The effective membrane area was 0.00181 m 2 . A schematic diagram of the DCMD apparatus is presented in our previous study [27,28]. Temperatures of feed and permeate solutions were maintained at 50 o C and 20 o C, respectively, and measured at both the inlet and outlet of the cell. The operating pressure and flow rate of the feed and permeate solutions were measured. Artificial brines and D.I. water were prepared and used as feed and permeate solutions, respectively.  Water  flux  was  automatically  measured  from  the  overflowed permeate solution every 10 min. The conductivities of the bulk feed and permeate solutions were also recorded every 10 min to calculate salt rejection. Due to the limitations of the lab-scale MD apparatus, the rust from the apparatus needed to be washed out to prohibit pore clogging. In this study, the apparatus was washed with D.I. water after 100 h and then was tested for further concentration.

texts.orig: 2.7. Cost analysis
texts.text: 2.7. Cost analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The capital costs and operating costs of the membrane-based lithium recovery process were determined to assess the economic feasibility. To compare with the conventional method, calculations were performed with two different methods. The first was the same scale as the conventional method, which was denoted as Cscale, and the other was the same cost basis as the conventional method, which was denoted as Ccost. Cscale  assumed that the scale of the membrane-based process was the same as that of the conventional method and compared the capital costs and operating costs. Meanwhile, Ccost assumed that the cost expenditure of the membrane-based process was the same as that of the conventional method to compare the lithium concentration speed of the membranebased process. The cost of the membrane-based process was calculated with the capacity method [29 -31], which is widely used for cost estimations based on a reduction in the specific costs with increasing capacity.  The  costs  for  any  capacity  C  were  estimated  with  degression coefficient m, reference costs Cref, and reference capacity Kref following Equation (2).

texts.orig: Cref, Kref, and m for the membrane-based process were summarized from the literature [29] (see Table S1). Membrane performance was also simulated through open-source software, which aided and adjusted the lab-scale MD performance to proper industrial-scale MD performance according to practical conditions, such as module size, flow rate, and temperature polarization [32,33] (see Table S2). Through comprehensive cost estimation using the capacity method and computer simulation,  the  costs  of  the  membrane-based  process  were  calculated  and compared to those of the conventional method.

texts.orig: After  reaching  900  ppm  lithium,  the  MD  process  started  to  fail because of crystal formation of divalent ions on the membrane surface, inducing  membrane  wetting  (Fig.  2c).  However,  before  this  critical point, the permeate conductivity did not show any significant change (see Fig. S3 in ESI y ). This result indicates that ions in brines could not penetrate the membrane toward the permeate, and the brine was successfully  concentrated  without  loss  of  lithium  ions.  Furthermore, deionized water is obtained as a by-product of permeate in this process.

texts.orig: As shown in Fig. 2d, to predict how and when the crystals were formed  by  precipitation  of  saturated  ions,  simulation  results  from PHREEQC software  indicated  that  no  crystals  formed  until  the  feed reached 900 ppm lithium. However, after the brine was over 900 ppm, calcium-based crystals (anhydride and gypsum) started to precipitate. Interestingly, and as predicted from the PHREEQC simulation (Fig. 2d), some crystals covered the membrane surface after MD operation (Fig. 2e and f). All ion concentrations in the feed except calcium ions continued to increase during the MD test. In contrast, the calcium concentration decreased at the end of the test due to precipitation of crystals on the membrane  surface  (Fig.  2b).  Fig.  2f  shows  the  rod-shaped  crystals known as the representative shape of calcium sulphate crystals. The rodshaped  crystals  were  confirmed  by  surface  analysis  of  these  crystals (XRD, XPS, and EDX), and further details will be  discussed later  (in Fig. 4). Therefore, to further increase the lithium concentration to an extractable level, calcium ions should be removed from the MD feed.

texts.orig: 3.2. Enrichment of lithium by membrane distillation with nanofiltration
texts.text: 3.2. Enrichment of lithium by membrane distillation with nanofiltration

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With addition of NF process before MD, divalent ions in the brine were removed to increase lithium concentration and to suppress their precipitation  in  the  feed  solution  at  the  surface  of  MD  membrane (Fig. 3a) [35]. Three commercial NF membranes were screened based on water flux and rejection of ions (NE 40, NE 70, and NE 90). As shown in Fig. 3b and c, the NE 40 membrane showed the highest water flux with extremely low rejection ( < 5%) of divalent ions (Ca 2 þ and Mg 2 þ ).  In contrast, the NE 90 membrane exhibited the highest rejection for all ions, resulting in low selectivity between monovalent ions (Na þ and Li þ ) and divalent ions (Ca 2 þ and Mg 2 þ ). The water flux of NE 90 (7.3 L m 2 h 1 ) was not enough to be utilized in this system. For this reason, NE 70 was selected for pre-treatment of the brine because of its suitable water flux  (25.1  L  m 2  h 1 ),  reasonable  rejection  ( > 30%) of divalent ions (Ca 2 þ and Mg 2 þ ), and low rejection ( < 10%) of monovalent ions (Na þ and Li þ ). Composition of NF pre-treated artificial brine is summarized in Table 1. Compared to the nascent artificial brine, NF pre-treated artificial brine showed 40% lower concentrations of Ca 2 þ (from 100 ppm to 60 ppm) and Mg 2 þ (from 100 ppm to 60 ppm) but similar concentrations of Li þ (from 100 ppm to 92 ppm) and Na þ (from 10,000 ppm to 9200 ppm). The MD process then was conducted using the NF pre-treated artificial brine.  Although  the  initial  ion  conductivity  of  the  NF pre-treated artificial brine (37.7 mS cm 1 ) was slightly lower than that of the nascent artificial brine (41.0 mS cm 1 ), the brine was enriched similarly  to  in  the  previous  demonstration  (see  Fig.  S3  in  ESI y ).  MD apparatus should be washed out to remove rust after 100 h of operation, because of the limitation in our lab-scale MD apparatus and as reported in the literature (see the detailed Section 2.6.2.), [28]. After rust was washed from the apparatus and MD operation was continued, the water flux  instantly  recovered,  and  ion  concentration  was  slightly  diluted (Fig. 3d and e, respectively). Finally, after 140 h operation, Li þ in the brine was successfully concentrated from 100 ppm to 1200 ppm. However, the MD process was stopped because of formation of crystals on the membrane surface, resulting in rapid decrease in water flux and increase in permeate conductivity.

texts.orig: 3.3. Crystal analysis
texts.text: 3.3. Crystal analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As shown in Fig. 4, different types of crystals were observed on the membrane surface after MD operation with NF pre-treated brine. In the first demonstration without NF pre-treatment, the rod-shaped crystals were formed (Fig. 2f). In XPS analysis, Ca2p (347.8 eV), S2p (169.2 eV), and O1s  (532.1 eV) peaks were mainly detected, indicating anhydride (CaSO4) and gypsum (CaSO4 ⋅ 2H2O) crystals. Small Na1s (1071.8 eV) and Cl2p peaks  (293.0  eV)  were  also  detected,  suggesting  halite  (NaCl) crystals (Fig. 4a). XRD analysis showed strong, narrow peaks for gypsum crystals  (Fig.  4b)  at  11.7 � ,  20.8 � ,  23.6 � ,  29.3 � ,  31.3 � ,  and  33.6 � that correspond to the (020), ( 121), (031), ( 141), (121), and (022) faces of gypsum crystals, respectively [36]. These peaks were observed within an error range of about 0.1 � . Ca (9.1%), S (7.6%), and O (50.0%) elements were seen in the EDX mapping data (Fig. 4c). Based on these analyses of ion concentrations in brine, the reduced Ca 2 þ concentration (Fig.  2b)  could  be  explained  by  precipitation  of  gypsum  crystals (Fig. 2e). As predicted in Fig. 2d for precipitated crystals, anhydride and gypsum crystals were precipitated during the operation. However, since anhydride (CaSO4) tends to be metastable when in contact with water, crystals readily hydrated into gypsum (CaSO4 ⋅ 2H2O) when exposed to water [37,38]. As a result, although anhydride crystals were predicted in Fig. 2d, XRD and XPS showed no peaks for anhydride crystals.

texts.orig: On the other hand, in the demonstration with NF pre-treatment, only Na1s (1071.8 eV) and Cl2p (293.0 eV) peaks were observed in the crystals on XPS. In XRD analysis, peaks at 32.1 � and 46.0 � in Fig. 4b were (200) and (220) of halite crystals, respectively [36]. Moreover, Na (10.3%) and Cl (11.4%) were confirmed by EDX element mapping (see Fig. 4c). Formation  of  anhydride  and  gypsum  crystals  was  inhibited  on  the membrane surface by using the NF pre-treated brine as feed, since NF had already removed divalent ions from the brine. However, formation of halite crystals consisting of monovalent ions (Na þ and Cl ) was not suppressed  because  NF  membrane  treatment  only  removed  divalent ions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.4. Techno-economic analysis
texts.text: 3.4. Techno-economic analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the present membrane process to be feasible, techno-economic analysis should be conducted in terms of capital and operating costs. The capital cost covers product construction costs to concentrate lithium ponds and to install chemical plants to recover the lithium product. The operating costs indicate annual costs spent for operating the whole facility,  labour,  and  electricity  costs.  Cost  analysis  of  the  conventional method refers to the technical report conducted by 'Lithium America ' in 2019 that analysed the production of lithium carbonate on a 25,000 ton per year scale in Bolivia [22]. The estimated capital and operating costs of the NF-MD process were compared with those of solar evaporation. Details  of  the  economic  estimation  were  followed  by  the  capacity method as described in Section 2.7 [29]. Beyond the expectation of easy installation, solar evaporation ponds consume 30% (129 million dollars) of the total capital costs (425 million dollars) because they require a large footprint and need to be coated entirely with hydrophobic materials  to  prevent  water  impregnation.  In  contrast,  as  in  Fig.  5,  the membrane-based process, assuming the same brine treatment size as that of solar evaporation, needs only about 18.6 million dollars, which is about 14% of the capital cost of solar evaporation. On the other hand, the  membrane-based  lithium  recovery  process  has  slightly  higher operating costs (108%, 67.6 million dollars per year) than the conventional  process  (100%,  62.4  million  dollars  per  year)  due  to  thermal energy and membrane replacement costs. The heat energy required for MD operation was calculated from an MD pilot plant study, assuming that 85% of the heat energy could be recovered using a heat exchanger [39]. Moreover, note that the operating cost of MD could be reduced through  utilizing waste heat and  low-quality energy which  are consumed in industrial processes [40,41]. Eighty percent of the membrane was assumed to be replaced every 5 years.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  summary,  we  introduced  a  membrane-based  lithium  recovery process from artificial lithium-containing brine based on the representative lithium ponds around the world. MD was selected to enrich the lithium from brine, whereas NF pre-treatment was required to remove divalent ions (Ca 2 þ and Mg 2 þ ), which were prone to form crystals. As a result, the membrane-based lithium recovery process could concentrate the artificial lithium-containing brine by 12-fold (from 100 ppm to 1200 ppm) which is an appropriate concentration to produce high-quality lithium by incorporating with other technologies such as precipitation, ion-exchange, liquid-liquid extraction, adsorption, and electrodialysis. We demonstrated that water flux from the membrane-based process was 60 times higher than that of solar evaporation, indicating a much lower capital cost and similar annual operating cost of the membrane process than those of solar evaporation. Due to development and the advantages of  membrane technology, such as the possibility of scale-up, a small footprint, and a weather-independent process, this new approach can effectively recover lithium from brine where economic feasibility is an issue due to low lithium concentration and weather instability.
texts.text: In  summary,  we  introduced  a  membrane-based  lithium  recovery process from artificial lithium-containing brine based on the representative lithium ponds around the world. MD was selected to enrich the lithium from brine, whereas NF pre-treatment was required to remove divalent ions (Ca 2 þ and Mg 2 þ ), which were prone to form crystals. As a result, the membrane-based lithium recovery process could concentrate the artificial lithium-containing brine by 12-fold (from 100 ppm to 1200 ppm) which is an appropriate concentration to produce high-quality lithium by incorporating with other technologies such as precipitation, ion-exchange, liquid-liquid extraction, adsorption, and electrodialysis. We demonstrated that water flux from the membrane-based process was 60 times higher than that of solar evaporation, indicating a much lower capital cost and similar annual operating cost of the membrane process than those of solar evaporation. Due to development and the advantages of  membrane technology, such as the possibility of scale-up, a small footprint, and a weather-independent process, this new approach can effectively recover lithium from brine where economic feasibility is an issue due to low lithium concentration and weather instability.
